quotes.verbatim_quote:
The symboi (1 indicates a note to be played afteran x. Example: 7

3 fp
7 __ aoa

As with x’s, if more than one O appears, play them together (after the x’s).
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the 'O' diagram symbol as a note plucked after the strummed x-notes, establishing the sequencing rule for reading chord diagrams in this method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The naming of chords with x’s is really debatable. For instance:

kom? kemTfl
on

pie ora

Both approaches are fine and you will see both of them used, so don’t expect
consistency here. Why? Because as you will see, there are times when one
approach would be ridiculous, and vice versa.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives an explicit rule that chord naming with x's is intentionally inconsistent, alerting the reader not to treat one naming convention as authoritative.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
a) a slash through a number means major (Example: #= Major 7th); b) + means sharped 5th unless it comes before 11
(+11) in which case it means sharped 11th; c) sus.is an abbreviation for the word suspended which means that the 3rd

has been replaced with the 4th (it also has another meaning but you needn’t worry about it in this book).
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines three critical chord symbol conventions—major slash notation, augmented/sharp-11 disambiguation, and sus—that are essential for reading chord names throughout the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Many guitar players learn new chords quickly by relating them visually to chords they already know. Example:
Suppose you encounter the following chord for the first time and after playing it, you
a

decide that you like the sound enough to want to memorize it. Suggestion: try relating it visually to:
quotes.why_load_bearing: This introduces the first memorization strategy—anchoring new chord shapes to visually similar known chords—which is the method's primary technique for building chord vocabulary.

quotes.topic: Five neck areas for chord memorization
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Divide the neck up into five main areas for any major or minor chord and use these areas as reference points for
any chord that you learn. For instance, using the A major chord as a model, the five areas would be:
quotes.why_load_bearing: This introduces the five-position neck framework—a foundational concept in this method for organizing and memorizing all major and minor chord shapes across the fretboard.

quotes.topic: Fingerings apply to initial chord only
quotes.verbatim_quote:
If left hand fingerings are
indicated in a diagrarm which contains x’s or 01’s, these fingerings indicate the initial chord sound only unless otherwise
indicated.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This rule clarifies that fingering numbers in mixed diagrams govern only the first strummed chord voicing, preventing misapplication of fingerings to subsequent plucked notes.
